scenarios.scenario-detail:
CyberGuard Technologies, a multinational cybersecurity firm with 15,000+ employees across 25 countries, is implementing a comprehensive security infrastructure upgrade supporting classified government contracts and Fortune 500 clients. The Chief Information Security Officer established dynamic information flow control policies per AC-4.3 with automated threat detection systems monitoring global threat intelligence feeds and adjusting network security policies based on changing risk conditions. The organization deployed NIAP-approved protection profiles per SA-4.7 for all commercial security products including next-generation firewalls evaluated against Network Device Protection Profile v2.2, enterprise VPN solutions meeting IPsec Virtual Private Network Client Protection Profile v1.0, and database management systems validated against Database Management System Protection Profile v4.0. The IT Asset Manager implemented comprehensive software usage restrictions per CM-10 with automated license tracking systems monitoring all 50,000+ software installations across the enterprise, maintaining detailed compliance databases, and conducting monthly audits to ensure installed instances remain within licensed quantities. The Security Architecture Team designed trusted communications channels per SA-8.18 with end-to-end encryption using FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated cryptographic modules for all inter-component communications, implementing mutual authentication between all system endpoints, and establishing secure API gateways for third-party integrations. The Business Continuity Manager developed alternate processing site procedures per CP-2.6 with comprehensive transfer plans for all mission-critical functions to geographically distributed backup facilities, maintaining operational continuity capabilities, and establishing detailed restoration procedures. The Facilities Manager enforced enhanced physical access controls per PE-3.1 with biometric authentication systems for all data center access, implementing dual-factor authorization requirements, and maintaining comprehensive access logging. The organization established central management per PL-9 for standardized security controls across all systems, implementing automated policy distribution tools, and maintaining hybrid control documentation. The Network Engineering Team configured wireless access points per AC-18.5 with directional antennas and calibrated transmission power levels, conducting quarterly RF surveys to validate signal containment within organizational boundaries. The IT Operations team implemented separate storage per CP-9.3 with critical system backups stored in GSA-certified fire-rated containers and geographically distributed facilities maintaining 75-mile separation from primary data centers. The Vulnerability Management Team established correlation processes per RA-5.10 using automated tools to identify multi-hop attack vectors, performing analysis within established timeframes, and maintaining comprehensive attack path documentation. However, when the global threat intelligence system detected an elevated threat condition requiring immediate network policy adjustments, the automated response system experienced a processing delay and took 18 minutes to implement the required information flow control changes across all affected network segments, slightly exceeding the organization's 15-minute requirement for dynamic policy adjustments during threat level changes.

Policies referenced: policy_AC-4.3, policy_CM-10, policy_SA-8.18, policy_CP-2.6, policy_PE-3.1, policy_PL-9, policy_AC-18.5, policy_CP-9.3, policy_RA-5.10, policy_SI-10.5

scenarios.judged-compliant-reason: Violation of policy_AC-4.3 RULE-02: Information flow controls MUST automatically adjust within 15 minutes when threat level changes are detected. The scenario shows a 18-minute delay (18 > 15), which exceeds the required timeframe. This is a direct violation of the dynamic information flow control policy requirements.

However, during the organization's most recent quarterly wireless survey conducted by certified RF engineers using calibrated spectrum analyzers, the team discovered that signals from the executive conference room wireless access point were measuring -80 dBm at the building's eastern boundary, which required immediate attention from the network engineering team to adjust antenna positioning and reduce transmission power to meet organizational boundary containment requirements.

Policies referenced: policy_AC-4.3, policy_CM-10, policy_SA-8.18, policy_CP-2.6, policy_PE-3.1, policy_PL-9, policy_AC-18.5, policy_CP-9.3, policy_RA-5.10, policy_SI-10.5

Since the violation was discovered during the wireless survey and remediation was required (indicating it was non-compliant at discovery), this constitutes a policy violation.

CyberDefense Industries, a leading cybersecurity contractor with 18,500+ employees supporting critical infrastructure protection for government agencies and Fortune 100 corporations, deployed an enterprise-wide security architecture designed to meet the most stringent compliance requirements while maintaining operational efficiency across global operations. The organization implemented sophisticated dynamic information flow control policies per AC-4.3 with AI-powered threat detection systems that automatically adjust network security policies within 8 minutes of detecting threat level changes, utilizing advanced behavioral analytics and threat intelligence feeds to maintain optimal security posture while supporting mission-critical operations. The Security Architecture Team designed military-grade trusted communications channels per SA-8.18 implementing NSA Suite B cryptographic algorithms for all sensitive data transmissions, establishing secure communication protocols with comprehensive endpoint authentication, implementing perfect forward secrecy for all connections, and maintaining detailed security architecture documentation for all communication pathways. The Business Continuity Manager developed comprehensive alternate processing site procedures per CP-2.6 with detailed operational transfer plans covering all critical business functions, maintaining hot-standby capabilities at three geographically distributed backup facilities, implementing automated failover systems with sub-minute recovery times, and establishing complete primary site restoration procedures with documented testing protocols. The Chief Information Security Officer established enterprise-wide central management per PL-9 with standardized security control deployment across all organizational systems, implementing automated configuration management tools, maintaining consistent security baselines, and documenting detailed control inheritance relationships with comprehensive audit trails. The Network Engineering Team configured enterprise wireless infrastructure per AC-18.5 with military-specification directional antenna systems and precisely calibrated transmission power levels ensuring signals remain well below -90 dBm at all organizational boundaries, conducting monthly wireless surveys using professional-grade RF measurement equipment, and maintaining comprehensive electromagnetic compatibility documentation. The Vulnerability Management Team established advanced correlation processes per RA-5.10 using next-generation automated correlation platforms combined with expert threat analysis to identify complex multi-hop attack vectors spanning multiple vulnerability types, performing comprehensive analysis within 18 hours of scan completion, and maintaining detailed attack path documentation with risk scoring and remediation prioritization. During the organization's annual security control review, the audit team discovered that the trusted sources list for the primary customer data processing system had not been updated following the organization's standard quarterly review cycle, with the last documented review occurring 120 days prior, requiring immediate attention from the Security team to conduct the overdue review and update the approved sources list to maintain compliance with input validation requirements.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
CyberTech Defense Solutions, a major defense contractor with 12,000+ employees supporting classified government projects across 18 secure facilities, implemented a comprehensive security architecture for their new TOP SECRET/SCI development environment processing advanced weapons system designs. The Chief Security Officer established strict isolation boundaries per SC-3.3, ensuring their authentication module contained only essential security functions with nonsecurity code comprising just 15% of the total codebase, well below the 25% threshold, with all logging and debugging functions documented as operationally necessary and approved by security architects. The IT Security team deployed automated software control mechanisms per CM-7.4 using a deny-by-exception policy across all 450 development workstations, maintaining current lists of unauthorized software including specific gaming applications, peer-to-peer file sharing tools, and unapproved development frameworks, with quarterly reviews conducted every 60 days and real-time blocking preventing execution of prohibited programs while generating immediate security alerts. The Identity and Access Management team implemented address confirmation procedures per IA-12.5 for all 2,800 contractor registrations, delivering verification codes through separate SMS channels to phone numbers obtained from authoritative HR records, with codes expiring within 18 hours and requiring completion before any system access privileges were granted. Privileged administrators configured security policy filters per AC-4.11 across the network infrastructure, with 45 authorized administrators maintaining documented filter configurations supporting different organizational security policies for classified data handling, unclassified research activities, and contractor access restrictions, all aligned with approved organizational policies and tested before production deployment. The organization displayed security attributes per AC-16.5 on all classified documents transmitted to 120 secure printers and display systems, showing human-readable classifications like 'TOP SECRET//SCI//NOFORN' using standard DoD naming conventions with proper dissemination instructions visible on all outputs. Automated account management systems per AC-2.2 removed temporary contractor accounts within 2 hours of expiration and disabled emergency break-glass accounts within 18 hours of creation, with continuous monitoring generating alerts for any failed removal attempts and triggering manual remediation within 90 minutes. Physical security controls per PE-2.3 restricted unescorted access to server facilities housing TOP SECRET systems to only personnel with current TOP SECRET clearances verified through the DoD security database, with all maintenance contractors requiring continuous escort by cleared facility security officers who maintained visual supervision and documented all access in detailed visitor logs. The Security Operations Center conducted automated incident response testing per IR-3.1 using approved simulation tools every 60 days for critical systems, achieving 85% coverage of documented response procedures with realistic attack scenarios that stressed response capabilities, with test results analyzed and improvements implemented within 25 days of completion. Separation of duties controls per AC-5 ensured that security administrators managing access controls could not also administer audit functions on the same systems, database administrators required business approval and logging for production data access, and financial transaction processes required approval from two segregated roles with quarterly reviews of role assignments conducted by the CISO. Comprehensive contingency plans per CP-2 were maintained for all 75 critical systems with recovery time objectives of 48 hours, including current contact information updated every 60 days, coordination with incident response procedures, full security control restoration validation, and secure plan storage with appropriate access controls protecting against unauthorized disclosure.

Policies referenced: policy_SC-3.3, policy_CM-7.4, policy_IA-12.5, policy_AC-4.11, policy_AC-16.5, policy_AC-2.2, policy_PE-2.3, policy_IR-3.1, policy_AC-5, policy_CP-2
scenarios.judged-compliant-reason: Scenario complies with all referenced policies. SC-3.3: Authentication module nonsecurity code at 15% is below 25% threshold (RULE-04), all functions documented and approved. CM-7.4: Unauthorized software lists reviewed every 60 days (meets quarterly 90-day requirement), real-time blocking implemented. IA-12.5: Codes expire within 18 hours (meets 24-hour limit), delivered via separate SMS channel from HR records. AC-4.11: 45 privileged administrators with documented configurations aligned with approved policies. AC-16.5: Proper human-readable security attributes displayed using standard DoD conventions. AC-2.2: Temporary accounts removed within 2 hours (exceeds 4-hour requirement), emergency accounts disabled within 18 hours (exceeds 24-hour requirement).
